tillerworks:dye-not-enough: You need at least 1 litre of dye to paint this.
tillerworks:engine-already: An engine is already installed — remove it with a wrench.
tillerworks:wheels-already: Wheels are already fitted — remove them with a wrench.
tillerworks:drum-filled-can: Filled jerry can ({0} mB). Drum: {1} / {2} mB.
tillerworks:key-wrong: This key does not fit this vehicle.
tillerworks:vehicle-locked: The vehicle is locked — you need its key.
tillerworks:dismount-too-fast: Too fast to get out — slow down first.
tillerworks:jack-no-vehicle: No vehicle within reach of the jack.
tillerworks:need-jack: Raise the vehicle on a jack before changing its wheels or engine.
tillerworks:tillerworks-guide-overview-t:
Tillerworks
Motor vehicles, engines, fuel and towing for Vintage Story.

Tillerworks lets you build and run a working Tractor and a family of trailers - assemble the chassis, raise it on a jack to fit an engine and wheels, brew your own fuel, then haul, plant and fertilize your fields.

The workflow, start to finish
1. Build a vehicle at a Workstation.
2. Raise it on a Jack and fit an engine and wheels.
3. Brew fuel and fill the tank.
4. Drive and maintain it.
5. Hitch up trailers to haul, plant and fertilize.

Work through the sections in order - each step depends on the one before it.
tillerworks:tillerworks-guide-build-text:
Building a Vehicle
Everything starts at the Workstation.

Craft a Workstation and place it down. Right-click it to open the build menu, then pick the vehicle you want - the Tractor to begin with. The panel lists the materials the vehicle needs alongside what you are carrying; when every entry turns green, press Craft and the finished chassis spawns on top of the station.

A freshly built vehicle is bare: no engine, no wheels, so it will not move yet. To make it road-ready you must fit both, and that is done with the Jack.

Tip: the Workstation only consumes materials when you actually press Craft, so browse the vehicle list freely to plan what to gather.
tillerworks:tillerworks-guide-parts-text:
Engines, Wheels & the Jack
No shortcuts - you need a Jack to work on a vehicle.

The Jack
Craft a Jack - an iron-plate frame built around an electric engine - and place it beside your vehicle. Right-click the Jack with an empty hand to pump it up: the nearest vehicle snaps onto the piston, perfectly centered, and rises with a hiss. Right-click again to lower it back down.

A vehicle must be raised on a Jack before you can add or remove its engine or wheels. Try it with the vehicle on the ground and you will be told to raise it first.

Fitting and removing parts
With the vehicle jacked up, simply use an engine or wheel item on it to install that part. To take parts off, use a Wrench on the vehicle - it removes the engine first, then the wheels, dropping each one back so you can reuse it.

Engines
Engines come in three kinds - small, large and electric - each cast at three tiers: iron, meteoric iron and steel. Every vehicle needs a particular kind; the Tractor takes a Large engine. Higher tiers give more top speed and quicker acceleration.

Wheels
There are seven wheel sets - standard, sports, racing, off-road, snow, all-terrain and plastic. Different treads grip different ground better (snow tyres on ice, off-road on dirt), which changes how well the vehicle holds its speed over that terrain.
tillerworks:tillerworks-guide-fuel-text:
Making Fuel
From a lump of charcoal to a full tank.

Fuel is brewed in a barrel in two quick steps, then distilled into its usable form.

Step 1 - Fuel Slurry
Drop 1 Charcoal into a barrel holding 1 L of Water. It dissolves on contact into 1 L of Fuel Slurry, a milky intermediate - no sealing required.

Step 2 - Raw Fuel
Add 1 Raw Fat (animal fat) to that barrel of Fuel Slurry. It converts straight away into 1 L of Raw Fuel.

Step 3 - Distill into Fuel
Raw Fuel is not usable yet. Scoop it into a Condenser - the standard Vintage Story distillation apparatus, set up over a heat source - and distill it. The result is refined Fuel, roughly 3 L of Fuel for every 4 L of Raw Fuel.

Storing and carrying
Keep bulk Fuel in a Fuel Drum (the industrial drum holds far more). To refuel in the field, fill a Jerry Can from the drum, then right-click a vehicle with the can to pour Fuel into its tank.

Every ratio is 1:1 and forgiving - 1 charcoal makes 1 L of slurry, 1 fat turns 1 L of slurry into 1 L of raw fuel - so nothing is wasted.
tillerworks:tillerworks-guide-drive-text:
Driving & Maintenance
Behind the wheel.

Right-click a vehicle with an empty hand to climb into the driver's seat.

Controls
W / S - throttle forward and reverse
A / D - steer left and right
Shift - dismount

The front wheels turn as you steer and the tractor swings to follow their arc. You cannot climb out while moving quickly - slow to a near stop first, or you will be told you are going too fast.

Fuel
The engine only runs while there is Fuel in the tank. Top it up with a Jerry Can whenever it runs low.

Locking
Craft a Vehicle Key and right-click your vehicle with it to bind and lock it. From then on, only a driver carrying the matching key can start it. Right-click with the key again to toggle the lock on and off.

Maintenance
To swap a worn wheel set or upgrade the engine, raise the vehicle on a Jack again - the jack rule always applies.
tillerworks:tillerworks-guide-trailers-t:
Trailers & Towing
Haul, plant and fertilize.

Trailers are pulled behind a vehicle and follow it around corners.

Hitching up
First take a trailer by hand: Shift + right-click it on foot to grab and pull it. Then walk up to your vehicle and right-click the vehicle while you are pulling - the trailer hands off and hitches onto the back. You can chain several trailers into a train. Interact with a hitched trailer again to unhitch it.

Trailer types
Storage / Flatbed - a 27-slot mobile chest; right-click to open it.
Seeder - plants seeds across three lanes of tilled soil as you drive, drawing from its own inventory (or from a storage trailer further up the train).
Fertilizer - spreads fertilizer over three lanes just behind it, feeding N/P/K into the soil.
Fluid - carries liquids from place to place.

Towing a loaded trailer trims your acceleration a little - heavier loads take longer to get rolling.
